bibliographic.citation: Anna C. Paues, 'A Newly Discovered Manuscript of the Poema Morale,' Anglia 30, new series 18 (1907): 217–237.
bibliographic.title: A Newly Discovered Manuscript of the Poema Morale

entities.description: Paues's article introduces McClean 123, describes its contents, ownership, script and dialect, compares its Poema Morale copy with the six previously known versions, and prints the McClean text on pp. 227–237.
entities.label: Anna C. Paues, A Newly Discovered Manuscript of the Poema Morale (1907)

rights.basis: The article was published in 1907; Internet Archive marks the exact volume NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: Paues's 1907 article, transcription and editorial contribution

bibliographic.citation: Richard Morris, ed., Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century: From the Unique MS. B. 14. 52 in the Library of Trinity College, Cambridge, Second Series, EETS Original Series 53 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1873), 220–232.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.title: Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century

entities.description: Morris's edition of Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.52 includes the Poema Morale copy as A Moral Ode on printed pp. 220–232. Paues identifies this printed T text before comparing its order, readings and lineation with the new McClean copy.
entities.label: Richard Morris, Old English Homilies of the Twelfth Century, Second Series (1873)

rights.basis: The edition was published in London in 1873 and is in the public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Morris's 1873 edition, transcription and editorial contribution

access.accessMethod: Request a public Study Room appointment through the official enquiry form at least two weeks in advance and identify MS McClean 123.
access.format: Onsite consultation; non-flash research photography may be permitted subject to restrictions
access.provider: The Fitzwilliam Museum, University of Cambridge

access.accessMethod: Email the personal-study image order form to the Image Library and request one complete color digital reproduction of the whole current physical object: outer boards or covers, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and endleaves, every manuscript leaf including blanks and adjacent works, and head, tail and fore-edge views where offered. Identify the target separately as Poema Morale, ff. 115r–120r, and ask for a quote, file inventory, foliation crosswalk, permitted private storage terms and any separate redistribution or publication licence.
access.contact: licensing@fitzmuseum.cam.ac.uk; Image Library, The Fitzwilliam Museum, Trumpington Street, Cambridge CB2 1RB, United Kingdom; +44 (0)1223 332927
access.format: Quoted digital image delivery; form lists JPEG/TIFF supply and per-photograph fees but a complete-codex price requires an institutional quotation

localCopies.label: Fitzwilliam personal-study image order form (2025)
notes: Supporting request document only; this is not a manuscript facsimile.

localCopies.label: Fitzwilliam Image Library terms and conditions (2024)
notes: Supporting rights document only; this is not a manuscript facsimile.
Requesting the whole object, rather than only ff. 115r–120r, is essential for binding, codicology, blank and adjacent-work context.
No supplied manuscript images are present in the cache. The two downloaded local copies are request and rights evidence documents, not manuscript facsimiles.
Private study supply does not permit publication, Git redistribution, print-on-demand or commercial reuse. Obtain a separate written licence for any such use.
access.provider: The Fitzwilliam Museum, University of Cambridge

rights.basis: The personal-study form says copyright in supplied images remains with the Fitzwilliam Museum and no supplied image may be published or reproduced without prior approval and a licence; the current Image Library terms grant only quotation-specific, non-transferable uses and require secure storage.
rights.component: Any manuscript images supplied by the Fitzwilliam Museum Image Library
rights.jurisdiction: Provider contract and applicable copyright law
notes: A private-study order is not permission to commit facsimiles to Git, publish them in the viewer, sublicense them, use them in commercial print-on-demand, or redistribute them.
If the museum supplies a complete facsimile, retain it only in the ignored private cache until the exact quotation and licence are recorded.

access.accessMethod: Use the Biblissima and ARLIMA records for discovery, then request images from the Fitzwilliam Museum. Do not treat generic Mirador interface chrome as an item facsimile.
access.format: Metadata-only discovery records; no verified item-specific image manifest
notes: Dated negative search scope: Fitzwilliam collection search, Biblissima/Biblissima IIIF interface, ARLIMA, exact shelfmark and Nuneaton Codex web searches, and IIIF/facsimile queries on 2026-07-12.
This finding does not prove the manuscript has never been digitized or that no internal photography exists; it records only that no real complete public object could be opened.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: This dates the physical witness, not the Poema Morale's composition.
dateStatements.value: Late thirteenth century or around 1300; Biblissima reports 1275–1325

dateStatements.label: Nuneaton ownership
dateStatements.notes: Ownership inscriptions associate the codex with women and the convent of Nuneaton; they do not prove production there.

entities.description: A late-thirteenth- or circa-1300 multilingual parchment miscellany. Its final textual item is a distinctive 335-line Poema Morale version at ff. 115r–120r, preceded by two lines from Sinners Beware; f. 114v records names of English letterforms. Fifteenth-century Nuneaton ownership does not establish where the codex or poem was composed.

holding.notes: No repository-owned exact item page was found in the current Fitzwilliam collection search; Biblissima, ARLIMA, DIMEV and the museum's published 1912 catalogue independently identify the holding and shelfmark.

entities.label: Cambridge, Fitzwilliam Museum, MS McClean 123

physicalDescription.extent: 122 folios; Paues identifies ff. 121–122 as flyleaves
notes: James describes old red skin over boards, mended, with the clasp gone; Paues calls it the original Morocco-covered boards.
The text is a multilingual miscellany with French, Latin and Middle English materials; the Poema Morale copy is the last substantive item.

Paues calls the relevant scribe French. Her dialect analysis calls M Kentish with strong western admixture, while DIMEV relays LAEME localization at grid 394 213 in central Gloucestershire; these witness-language claims remain attributed and are not transferred to the abstract work.

rights.basis: The work and manuscript copy predate modern copyright by centuries; U-M also applies No Copyright–United States to the CME collection materials.
rights.component: The medieval textual content of Poema Morale and the McClean 123 copy

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: Hill dates this copy before 1200; the current Trinity catalogue assigns the codex to the thirteenth century.
dateStatements.value: Late twelfth or early thirteenth century

entities.description: The witness underlying Morris's 1873 printed T text, whose line order Paues cites in italics at the right of the McClean text. The current Trinity catalogue places the poem at ff. 2r–9v and the next sermon at f. 10r; Paues does not establish that she directly consulted the codex.

next_steps: Submit the official personal-study image order form for the whole physical object, naming every board, spine, pastedown, endleaf, blank, manuscript leaf and edge where offered.
Map the work separately as ff. 115r–120r and ask for an image inventory and foliation crosswalk.
Request separate written terms for private cache retention, public Git distribution, noncommercial publication, commercial print-on-demand and sublicensing; do not infer any of these from delivery.
openQuestions.question: Does the Fitzwilliam Museum already hold or permit creation of a complete color digital facsimile of MS McClean 123, and what exact private-storage, research, Git-publication, print-on-demand and commercial permissions would govern it?

next_steps: Inspect the exact LAEME corpus profile and its text-language versus scribal-language methodology.
Consult post-1977 dialectological work on McClean 123 before assigning a single modern dialect region.
Keep both claims at the witness layer and do not use either as proof of the abstract work's composition place.
openQuestions.question: How should Paues's description of the McClean copy as Kentish with strong western admixture be reconciled with DIMEV's LAEME grid 394 213 localization in central Gloucestershire?

next_steps: Inspect f. 115r and the facing f. 114v in a complete facsimile for layout, ruling, initial and spacing evidence.
Compare the two lines with the full Jesus College MS 29 Sinners Beware text and later textual scholarship.
Continue to exclude the two lines from the 335-line McClean Poema Morale count while preserving them in the encoded-source boundary.
openQuestions.question: Were the two Sinners Beware lines before the McClean Poema Morale copied accidentally from an exemplar containing both works, or intentionally used as a motto?

next_steps: Ask U-M for item-level production metadata and the source image package used for keying.
Confirm whether the 155b folio error entered at initial keying or a later transformation.
Treat TEMPHEAD as evidence of a 2016 validation pass, not as the creation date.
openQuestions.question: What source images, initial keying date, correction history and item-level production documentation underlie U-M CME00070 before the documented 2016 review?

assertion.notes: The record itself warns that inherited bibliographic data contain contradictions.

relations.subjectLabel: Biblissima+ record for Cambridge, Fitzwilliam Museum, McClean 123

reviewNotes: The intellectual work Poema Morale, the distinctive McClean 123 witness version, Paues's article and transcription, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML are separate layers.

Paues identifies T as the Trinity B.14.52 copy printed by Morris in 1873 and gives corresponding T line numbers, but does not establish direct consultation of the codex. Her comparison is therefore routed through Morris's printed edition; the modern Trinity facsimile remains supporting witness evidence.

Paues labels the second manuscript surface f. 115b. The repository XML instead has MILESTONE N="155b"; the printed page image and the surrounding 115a, 116a sequence establish this as an encoding-layer transcription error, not a second folio.

Paues says the manuscript's first two lines are the opening of Sinners Beware and considers either copying error or a deliberately placed motto. DIMEV consequently treats them as a verse-couplet heading before the Poema Morale witness.

The Biblissima page carries generic IIIF and Mirador interface language, but its live control says that there is no digitized object in the selection and supplies no item-specific Presentation manifest. No public McClean 123 facsimile was recorded.

The Trinity bundle contains every one of the provider's 216 published canvases. It includes covers and insides, four flyleaves, manuscript leaves, stubs and ten UV alternates; no spine or edge views are exposed, so complete means complete published inventory rather than proven exhaustive physical-object photography.

The reused Trinity cache has the same device and inode as the existing CME00035 bundle. Its inventory records 214 exact 1800,-width image requests and two native full-image stub requests; sampled ordinary, target, stub and final images match the recorded profiles and dimensions.

The Fitzwilliam personal-study order form permits ordering images for research but says supplied images may not be published or reproduced without prior approval and a licence. Private cache possession therefore would not authorize Git redistribution, print-on-demand, or commercial use.

lineage.summary: CME00070 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic selection from Anna C. Paues's 1907 Anglia article A Newly Discovered Manuscript of the Poema Morale. The encoding transcribes only the edited text on printed pp. 227–237, not Paues's introduction on pp. 217–226. Paues directly transcribed the newly identified copy in Cambridge, Fitzwilliam Museum, MS McClean 123, ff. 115r–120r, and aligned its 335 Poema Morale lines against Richard Morris's 1873 printed edition of the Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.52 copy; the two opening encoded lines belong to Sinners Beware rather than Poema Morale. No evidence was found that Paues directly consulted the Trinity codex. No item-specific complete McClean 123 image object was found: Biblissima's generic Mirador control explicitly reports no digitized object, while current scholarly records say the manuscript must be seen onsite. The record therefore preserves Fitzwilliam onsite and whole-object reproduction routes instead of inventing facsimile access. The requested reproduction should include boards or covers, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, every leaf and edges where offered, with the target mapped separately to ff. 115r–120r. A complete public 216-canvas Trinity comparison object is reused by true hard link and mapped to ff. 2r–9v / canvases 13–28 as a modern supporting source. Complete public copies of the Anglia volume, the standalone Paues article, Morris's complete 1873 volume, the 1912 McClean catalogue, Hill's 1977 study, and DIMEV v1.0 are also cached with exact source links.

catalogSummary.date: Poema Morale: late twelfth century; McClean composite copy: late thirteenth century or c. 1300

catalogSummary.title: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)

catalogingSubject.label: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the physically continuous 337-line selection that Anna C. Paues transcribed from Fitzwilliam Museum MS McClean 123, fols. 115r–120r, and that CME00070 encodes from her 1907 edition. Paues identifies the first two copied lines as the opening of the separate work Sinners Beware; the following 335 lines are the McClean version of Poema Morale. The record therefore treats the encoded unit as a composite rather than silently assigning all 337 lines to Poema Morale. It excludes the rest of McClean 123, Paues's introductory study and apparatus as modern authorship, Trinity College MS B.14.52 as a comparison witness, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact as distinct physical, editorial, and digital layers.

contentParts.label: Opening couplet from Sinners Beware

locators.value: f. 115r, first two copied lines immediately before Poema Morale

notes: The fragment is physically continuous with the following Poema Morale text; this part boundary is analytical, not a claim of a separate leaf, rubric, or XML division.
DIMEV indexes the complete Sinners Beware separately as NIMEV 3607 / record-5698 and describes it as fifty-nine six-line stanzas; CME00070 contains only these two opening lines.
contentParts.summary: A two-line invocation asking the Holy Ghost for guidance and protection. Paues identifies it as the opening of Sinners Beware and discusses its possible placement as either scribal error or motto.

contentParts.label: Poema Morale: McClean 123 version

locators.value: pp. 227–237, beginning after the two-line opening couplet
ff. 115r–120r, beginning immediately after the two-line opening couplet

notes: Paues's italic figures at the right align selected M lines with Trinity witness T; they do not make T the base text.
DIMEV's 398-line extent describes the abstract work in its fullest witnesses, not this shorter 335-line version.
contentParts.summary: The 335-line M version of the anonymous late-twelfth-century moral verse homily, beginning 'Ich am elder þane ich was of wintre & of lore' and ending with a prayer to reach Christ after death.

assertion.notes: This dates the abstract work, not McClean 123. Hill reports earlier metrical reasoning that the original could not predate approximately 1170–1200 and gives her own view that it was first written during 1154–1189.

dateStatements.display: Late twelfth century; Hill proposed 1154–1189, while an earlier metrical argument allowed approximately 1170–1200

assertion.notes: This dates the surviving copy in which the two-line Sinners Beware opening and the 335-line Poema Morale version are physically continuous; it does not date either abstract work's composition.

dateStatements.display: Late thirteenth century or around 1300 in Fitzwilliam Museum MS McClean 123

assertion.notes: This is the first publication of the newly discovered McClean version and its attached opening couplet, not the first publication of Poema Morale from any witness.

dateStatements.display: First published by Anna C. Paues in Anglia 30 (1907), pp. 227–237

extent.description: Two conceptually distinct verse components represented continuously in one XML LG: 2 lines from the opening of Sinners Beware plus the 335-line McClean Poema Morale version. The 337-line total describes CME00070 and this witness selection, not the abstract Poema Morale, which DIMEV describes as 398 lines in couplets in its fullest form.

formStatements.description: Both components are verse. CME00070 contains one undivided LG with 337 L elements: a two-line opening fragment from Sinners Beware followed by 335 Poema Morale lines; DIMEV describes the abstract Poema Morale as 398 lines in rhyming couplets across its fullest witnesses.

assertion.notes: Hill explicitly calls the work a verse-sermon; verse homily is the normalized corpus label.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The language of the McClean copy is a witness-level realization. Conflicting Kentish/Western, Essex, and central Gloucestershire localizations are not promoted here to the abstract work.

Paues's italic letters represent expanded abbreviations or supplied text, while italic figures at the right map M to Trinity witness T; neither convention should be interpreted as modern semantic emphasis.
The repository XML milestone 155b is a transcription or encoding error: Paues's printed page 228 labels the corresponding manuscript folio 115b, modernized here as f. 115v.

The fifteenth-century Nuneaton ownership inscriptions explain the names Nuneaton Book and Nuneaton Codex but do not prove that the manuscript, Poema Morale, or Sinners Beware was composed or copied at Nuneaton.

The complete Trinity B.14.52 facsimile is a comparison source only. The direct physical source for the Paues/CME text remains McClean 123, for which no complete public item-specific image object was found as of the review date.
Any newly supplied McClean images should be cached as the complete obtainable manuscript object and mapped to ff. 115r–120r, rather than downloading or preserving only the work portion.

next_steps: Review scholarship after Hill on the earliest T witness, metre, rhyme and dialect layers.
Keep composition claims separate from the language and production places of individual copies.
openQuestions.question: Can the original Poema Morale be dated or localized more securely than late-twelfth-century composition and Hill's tentative southern South-East Midlands reconstruction around Middlesex or London?

next_steps: Inspect a complete high-resolution facsimile of McClean 123 for mise-en-page, ruling, initials, spacing, punctuation and any rubric around the transition on f. 115r.
Compare the couplet with the complete Sinners Beware witness and later textual scholarship.
openQuestions.question: Were the two Sinners Beware lines before the McClean Poema Morale copied accidentally from an exemplar containing both works, or intentionally used as a suitable motto or heading?

next_steps: Consult the live LAEME corpus entry and later linguistic scholarship for McClean 123.
Do not transfer any witness-language localization to the abstract work's composition place without explicit textual argument.
openQuestions.question: How should the McClean copy's witness language be localized given Paues's Kentish-with-Western-admixture description, Hill's discussion of South-Eastern or Essex features, and DIMEV's LAEME-derived central Gloucestershire grid reference?

next_steps: Ask the Fitzwilliam Image Library for a whole-object quotation covering covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and endleaves, every manuscript leaf including blanks and adjacent works, and edges where offered.
Identify ff. 115r–120r separately as the target portion and request an image-inventory-to-folio crosswalk.
Request separate written terms for private research storage, public repository redistribution, and commercial or print-on-demand reuse before publishing supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete color digital facsimile of McClean 123 be obtained and what exact permissions would govern private research, public Git redistribution and commercial print-on-demand use?

notes: Both represented components are Middle English religious verse. This broad cultural-origin statement does not identify a precise composition site.

assertion.notes: Hill reconstructed the original version on the southern border of the South-East Midland dialect area around Middlesex or London. This is a critical localization, not documentary proof.

placeStatements.placeLabel: Southern South-East Midlands (Middlesex or London area)

notes: Hill reports that Paues transcribed the manuscript in February 1905.

scope.description: Direct transcription of the physically continuous text at McClean 123, fols. 115r–120r
Textual comparison of the McClean version with Morris's printed T text and other published Poema Morale copies; direct consultation of manuscripts other than McClean 123 is not established

subjects.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor

summaries.value: After a two-line invocation borrowed from or shared with the opening of Sinners Beware, the McClean Poema Morale speaker reflects on age, wasted life, repentance, charity, transient wealth, death, judgment, hell, heaven, and the need to live well before mercy is sought too late. The homiletic argument contrasts temporary earthly pleasure and possessions with eternal punishment or salvation and closes with a prayer for a good life and end.

Part 1 is a physically attached two-line opening from Sinners Beware. Part 2 is the 335-line McClean version of Poema Morale, one of seven complete witnesses indexed by DIMEV; the abstract work reaches 398 lines in its fullest copies.

CME00070 encodes Paues's printed text on pp. 227–237 only. It omits her introductory study on pp. 217–226 and does not reproduce McClean 123 outside fols. 115r–120r. Within that selected text the XML preserves all 337 L elements, Paues's footnotes and marginal comparison notes, printed page milestones, manuscript folio milestones, local line figures, and italic Trinity-comparison line figures.

The two-line Sinners Beware fragment and 335-line Poema Morale version are conceptually separate content parts but not separate leaves or XML divisions: they run continuously from f. 115r within a single LG. Paues considered the first couplet either a copying mistake from an exemplar containing both works or a deliberately suitable motto; DIMEV calls it a verse-couplet heading. The record preserves that uncertainty.

assertion.notes: The qualifier is supplied to describe the actual composite electronic unit; it is not a historical manuscript title.

titles.value: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)

notes: This is the conventional scholarly title, not an authorial or manuscript title.

assertion.notes: Betty Hill proposed this descriptive title in 1977; Poema Morale remains the conventional identifier used by Paues and DIMEV.

notes: Historical English title reported in Hill's discussion of the work's names.

titles.value: Ich am elder þane ich was of wintre & of lore

notes: The selected two lines are the opening of the separately indexed 59-stanza work; Paues's title is retained for the fragment.

titles.value: Þe holi gostes miȝte, us alle helpe & diȝte, us wissie & us teche
